conditions.name: Acne Vulgaris
conditions.description: Chronic inflammatory disease of the pilosebaceous unit affecting 85% of adolescents and 15% of adults. Characterized by comedones, papules, pustules, nodules, cysts, and potential scarring.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH) more common in Fitzpatrick III-VI; scarring risk higher in V-VI. Nodulocystic acne more common in SOC.

rootCauses.description: Abnormal desquamation of keratinocytes leading to microcomedone formation
Androgen-driven sebaceous gland hyperplasia; DHT is primary mediator
IA1 phylotype triggers inflammatory cascade via TLR2/TLR4
Innate immune response to C. acnes and biofilm formation
High glycemic load increases IGF-1, stimulating sebum production
Gut-skin axis; dysbiosis may drive systemic inflammation
Hyperinsulinemia increases androgens and IGF-1
CRH/ACTH axis increases cortisol and androgens, stimulating sebum

products.name: Benzoyl peroxide 2.5-5%
products.description: Antimicrobial and comedolytic; first-line for mild-moderate acne

products.name: Topical retinoids (adapalene, tretinoin)
products.description: Normalize desquamation, reduce comedones; cornerstone of acne therapy

products.name: Salicylic acid (BHA)
products.description: Keratolytic and anti-inflammatory; good for comedonal acne

products.name: Azelaic acid 15-20%
products.description: Anti-inflammatory and anti-comedonal; safe for pregnancy; benefits PIH in darker skin

supplements.name: Zinc picolinate 25-30mg
supplements.description: Inhibits 5-alpha reductase, anti-inflammatory; pair with copper 2-3mg

supplements.name: Omega-3 (EPA/DHA 1000-2000mg)
supplements.description: Anti-inflammatory; reduces PGE2 and LTB4; improves insulin sensitivity

supplements.name: Vitamin D3 2000-4000 IU
supplements.description: Deficiency common in acne; immune modulation; check 25(OH)D levels

practitioner.name: Dermatologist referral
practitioner.description: For moderate-severe acne, nodulocystic acne, or acne not responding to OTC treatment

practitioner.name: Endocrinologist referral
practitioner.description: If hormonal acne suspected (jawline distribution, menstrual flares, hirsutism)

basys_health.name: Comprehensive hormone panel
basys_health.description: DUTCH test for hormonal acne; checks DHEA-S, testosterone, cortisol, estrogen metabolites

basys_health.name: Gut microbiome test
basys_health.description: GI-MAP or similar; identifies dysbiosis contributing to systemic inflammation

conditions.name: Hormonal Acne
conditions.description: Acne driven by hormonal fluctuations, particularly androgens, DHEA-S, and hormonal changes during menstrual cycle, pregnancy, or menopause. Often presents as deep, painful cysts along jawline, chin, and neck.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: Higher PIH risk in darker skin; may coexist with melasma in Fitzpatrick IV-VI

rootCauses.description: DHEA-S, testosterone, DHT drive sebum production
Mid-cycle and premenstrual estrogen drops worsen acne
PCOS-related; increases androgens and IGF-1
Stress increases cortisol which increases androgens
Estrobolome dysfunction affects estrogen metabolism

products.name: Azelaic acid 20%
products.description: Anti-inflammatory; blocks androgen receptors in sebaceous glands

products.name: Spironolactone (prescription)
products.description: Androgen receptor blocker; first-line for hormonal acne in women

supplements.name: Spearmint tea
supplements.description: Anti-androgen properties; 2 cups daily showed reduction in free testosterone

supplements.name: Inositol (myo-inositol)
supplements.description: Improves insulin sensitivity; especially beneficial for PCOS-related acne

supplements.name: DIM (diindolylmethane)
supplements.description: Promotes estrogen metabolism toward beneficial 2-hydroxyestrone pathway

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For prescription spironolactone, hormonal acne management

practitioner.name: Endocrinologist or OB/GYN
practitioner.description: For PCOS evaluation, hormonal testing, birth control options

basys_health.name: DUTCH Complete hormone panel
basys_health.description: Comprehensive assessment of cortisol, DHEA-S, testosterone, estrogen metabolites, progesterone

basys_health.name: Fasting insulin and glucose
basys_health.description: Assess insulin resistance as driver of androgen excess

basys_health.name: Thyroid panel
basys_health.description: Rule out thyroid dysfunction affecting hormones

conditions.name: Acne Mechanica
conditions.description: Acne triggered by friction, pressure, heat, or occlusion from clothing, equipment, or physical contact. Common in athletes, mask-wearers, and people with occupations requiring helmets or tight gear.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: PIH risk in darker skin from friction and inflammation

rootCauses.description: Physical irritation disrupts follicular epithelium
Traps sweat and sebum, promoting bacterial growth
Compresses follicles and increases inflammation

products.name: Benzoyl peroxide 2.5-5%
products.description: Antimicrobial; reduces C. acnes on occluded skin

products.name: Salicylic acid cleanser
products.description: Keratolytic; prevents follicular plugging from occlusion

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: If not improving with behavioral modifications and OTC treatment

conditions.name: Fungal Acne (Malassezia Folliculitis)
conditions.description: Folliculitis caused by Malassezia yeast overgrowth, often misdiagnosed as acne vulgaris. Presents as monomorphic papules and pustules, typically on chest, back, and forehead.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: PIH risk in darker skin from inflammation; misdiagnosis common

rootCauses.description: Lipophilic yeast thrives in sebum-rich environments
Promotes yeast proliferation
Disrupts bacterial balance, allowing yeast overgrowth
Reduced immune control of commensal yeast

products.name: Ketoconazole 2% shampoo
products.description: Antifungal; use as body wash on affected areas

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For oral antifungal (fluconazole/itraconazole) if topical fails

basys_health.name: Gut microbiome assessment
basys_health.description: Systemic fungal overgrowth may contribute

conditions.name: Steroid Acne
conditions.description: Acneiform eruption caused by systemic corticosteroids, anabolic steroids, or topical steroid overuse. Presents as sudden onset of monomorphic inflammatory papules.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: PIH risk in darker skin; steroid-induced atrophy also a concern

rootCauses.description: Prednisone and similar trigger sebaceous hyperplasia
Exogenous androgens increase sebum production
Perioral and facial acneiform eruption

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: Essential for diagnosis and management; may need to adjust steroid regimen

practitioner.name: Prescribing physician
practitioner.description: Discuss steroid taper or alternatives

conditions.name: Rosacea
conditions.description: Chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by facial erythema, papules, pustules, and telangiectasias. Triggers include heat, alcohol, spicy foods, and stress.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: Underdiagnosed in darker skin; erythema may appear as brown or violaceous discoloration; burning/stinging sensation is key diagnostic clue

rootCauses.description: Abnormal blood vessel reactivity and inflammation
Demodex folliculorum density higher in rosacea patients
Family history common; Celtic/Northern European ancestry
Chronic sun exposure damages vessels and skin
H. pylori and SIBO associations reported
Stress triggers flushing and neurogenic inflammation

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For oral antibiotics (doxycycline, minocycline) or isotretinoin for severe cases

basys_health.name: Gut microbiome test
basys_health.description: Assess for SIBO, dysbiosis linked to rosacea

basys_health.name: Food sensitivity testing
basys_health.description: Identify trigger foods

conditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)
conditions.description: Chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin condition characterized by pruritus, erythema, and barrier dysfunction. Often associated with asthma and allergic rhinitis (atopic triad).
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: In darker skin, appears as violaceous, gray, or dark brown patches; lichenification more prominent; follicular prominence common

rootCauses.description: Filaggrin mutations; ceramide deficiency; increased TEWL
IL-4, IL-5, IL-13 drive inflammation and pruritus
Dust mites, pollen, pet dander trigger flares
S. aureus overgrowth; reduced microbial diversity
Leaky gut may increase systemic inflammation
Stress worsens symptoms via HPA axis and immune modulation
Dairy, eggs, wheat, soy in some patients

products.name: Ceramide-rich moisturizers
products.description: Restore barrier; 2:4:2 cholesterol:ceramides:fatty acids ratio optimal

products.name: Topical corticosteroids
products.description: Anti-inflammatory; use appropriate potency for body area

products.name: Calcineurin inhibitors
products.description: Tacrolimus/pimecrolimus; steroid-sparing for face and folds

supplements.name: Vitamin D3
supplements.description: Deficiency common in eczema; immune modulation

supplements.name: Quercetin
supplements.description: Natural mast cell stabilizer; antihistamine properties

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For moderate-severe disease; prescription topicals; phototherapy

practitioner.name: Allergist
practitioner.description: For patch testing, food allergy evaluation

basys_health.description: Assess dysbiosis and leaky gut markers

basys_health.name: Food sensitivity panel
basys_health.description: IgG-mediated food sensitivities

basys_health.name: Vitamin D level
basys_health.description: Check 25(OH)D status

conditions.name: Contact Dermatitis
conditions.description: Inflammatory skin reaction caused by contact with irritants or allergens. Divided into irritant contact dermatitis (80% of cases) and allergic contact dermatitis (20%).
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: In darker skin, erythema may not be visible; look for edema, warmth, and distribution pattern; PIH common after resolution

rootCauses.description: Detergents, solvents, acids, alkalis directly damage skin
Type IV hypersensitivity to specific allergens
Impaired barrier allows penetration of irritants/allergens

products.name: Barrier repair moisturizers
products.description: Ceramides, cholesterol, fatty acids to restore barrier

products.name: Calcineurin inhibitors
products.description: Steroid-sparing for face and chronic cases

supplements.name: Vitamin C
supplements.description: Supports barrier repair and antioxidant defense

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For patch testing, severe cases, occupational dermatitis

practitioner.name: Allergist
practitioner.description: For comprehensive allergy testing

conditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
conditions.description: Chronic, relapsing inflammatory condition affecting sebum-rich areas (scalp, face, chest). Associated with Malassezia yeast overgrowth and immune response.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: In darker skin, erythema may appear as darker brown or violaceous patches; hypopigmentation common post-treatment; SD can be misdiagnosed as tinea versicolor

rootCauses.description: Lipophilic yeast colonizes sebum-rich areas
Androgen-driven sebum production feeds yeast
Individual susceptibility to Malassezia antigens
Parkinson's disease increases risk
Psychological stress exacerbates symptoms

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For severe or refractory cases; oral antifungals may be needed

basys_health.name: Gut microbiome assessment
basys_health.description: Systemic yeast overgrowth may contribute

conditions.name: Psoriasis
conditions.description: Chronic, immune-mediated inflammatory disease characterized by well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale. Associated with significant comorbidities including psoriatic arthritis and cardiovascular disease.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: In darker skin, plaques appear violaceous, purple, or dark brown; erythema less visible; dyspigmentation can be more distressing than plaques; underdiagnosed due to atypical color

rootCauses.description: 30% family history; PSORS1 locus (HLA-Cw6) major susceptibility
TNF-α, IL-17, IL-23 are key cytokines
Infection, trauma, stress, medications
Leaky gut, SIBO, and dysbiosis linked to psoriasis
Adipokines promote inflammation; weight loss improves severity
Exacerbate psoriasis and reduce treatment response

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: Essential; phototherapy, systemic medications, biologics

practitioner.name: Rheumatologist
practitioner.description: If psoriatic arthritis present

basys_health.description: Assess metabolic syndrome, liver function before systemic therapy

conditions.name: Melasma
conditions.description: Acquired hyperpigmentation characterized by symmetric, blotchy brown patches on face. Triggered by UV exposure, hormonal factors, and genetic predisposition.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: Higher prevalence in Fitzpatrick IV-VI; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation risk with aggressive treatments; visible light contributes to pigmentation in darker skin

rootCauses.description: Stimulates melanocyte activity
Estrogen, progesterone; pregnancy, OCPs, HRT
Family history common; darker skin types
Contributes to pigmentation in darker skin
Autoimmune thyroid disease association

products.name: Hydroquinone 2-4%
products.description: Gold standard; limit to 3-4 months, then maintenance

products.name: Azelaic acid 20%
products.description: Safe for long-term; effective for darker skin

products.name: Tranexamic acid 2-5%
products.description: Topical; emerging gold standard for melasma

products.name: Tretinoin 0.025-0.1%
products.description: Increases cell turnover; enhances penetration of lightening agents

practitioner.description: For prescription options, chemical peels, laser (conservative in dark skin)

conditions.name: Post-Inflammatory Hyperpigmentation (PIH)
conditions.description: Acquired hyperpigmentation following cutaneous inflammation or injury. Represents melanin overproduction or incontinence in response to inflammatory mediators.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: Much more common and severe in Fitzpatrick IV-VI; prevention is paramount; PIH often more distressing than original condition in darker skin

rootCauses.description: IL-1, TNF-α, GM-CSF stimulate melanocytes
Inflammation triggers melanin production
Post-inflammatory skin is photosensitive; UV worsens PIH
Fitzpatrick III-VI at much higher risk

products.name: Retinoids
products.description: Increase cell turnover; prevent new acne that causes PIH

products.name: Tranexamic acid 2-5%
products.description: Topical; inhibits plasminogen; effective for PIH

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For persistent PIH; chemical peels (conservative), laser (Q-switched Nd:YAG in dark skin)

conditions.name: Solar Lentigines
conditions.description: Benign hyperpigmented macules caused by chronic UV exposure. Represent localized melanocyte proliferation with increased melanin deposition.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: Higher prevalence in Fitzpatrick I-III; in darker skin, may present as darker brown or gray-brown patches; higher PIH risk from procedures

rootCauses.description: Cumulative sun damage; UVA primarily responsible
Increased melanocyte number and melanin
UV-induced p53 mutations in keratinocytes

products.name: Tretinoin 0.05-0.1%
products.description: Increases cell turnover; used with HQ

products.name: Azelaic acid 20%
products.description: Alternative to HQ; safer for long-term

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For Q-switched laser (Nd:YAG 1064nm for dark skin), IPL, cryotherapy

conditions.name: Vitiligo
conditions.description: Autoimmune destruction of melanocytes resulting in depigmented (white) macules and patches. Associated with other autoimmune conditions.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: More psychologically distressing in darker skin due to higher contrast; phototherapy may be less effective in very dark skin; camouflage options important

rootCauses.description: CD8+ T-cells target melanocytes
Polygenic; 30% family history
Excessive H2O2 in epidermis; defective catalase
20-30% have autoimmune thyroid dysfunction
Trauma initiates new lesions

products.name: Tacrolimus 0.1% / Pimecrolimus 1%
products.description: Calcineurin inhibitors; T-cell suppression; face and folds

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: Essential for phototherapy, systemic therapy, surgical options

basys_health.name: Thyroid screening

basys_health.name: Autoimmune panel
basys_health.description: B12, fasting glucose, ANA if indicated

conditions.name: Actinic Keratosis
conditions.description: Pre-malignant lesion caused by chronic UV exposure. Intraepidermal keratinocyte dysplasia with potential to progress to squamous cell carcinoma.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: Higher prevalence in Fitzpatrick I-III; in darker skin, may present as darker hyperpigmented lesions; still requires sun protection

rootCauses.description: Cumulative DNA damage to keratinocytes
Organ transplant recipients at very high risk
Fair skin, family history

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: Essential for diagnosis and treatment; cryotherapy, photodynamic therapy, biopsy if suspicious

conditions.name: Skin Cancer (Melanoma)
conditions.description: Malignant tumor of melanocytes. Most aggressive form of skin cancer with high metastatic potential. Early detection is critical.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: In darker skin, melanoma often appears on palms, soles, nail beds, and mucous membranes (acral lentiginous melanoma); lower survival rates due to delayed diagnosis

rootCauses.description: Primary cause for most melanoma types
BRAF, NRAS, CDKN2A mutations
10% have familial melanoma
>50 common nevi increases risk
Organ transplant recipients at higher risk

practitioner.name: Dermatologist - URGENT
practitioner.description: Immediate referral for biopsy and treatment

conditions.name: Perioral Dermatitis
conditions.description: Inflammatory eruption around mouth, nose, and eyes. Often triggered by topical steroids, fluoride toothpaste, or cosmetic products.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: In darker skin, erythema may appear as darker brown or gray patches; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation common

rootCauses.description: Most common trigger; especially potent steroids on face
Can trigger or exacerbate
Heavy moisturizers, occlusive products
More common in women
C. acnes and Demodex implicated

products.name: Discontinue topical steroids
products.description: Gradual taper; steroid withdrawal may worsen initially

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For oral antibiotics (doxycycline, minocycline) if topical fails

conditions.name: Keratosis Pilaris
conditions.description: Common benign condition characterized by keratin plugs within hair follicles, creating rough papules. Often called 'chicken skin.'
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: In darker skin, post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation may occur around lesions; papules may be more visible

rootCauses.description: Excess keratin blocks hair follicles
Autosomal dominant; often familial
Worse in winter; improves with humidity
Associated with atopic dermatitis

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: If severe or not responding to OTC treatment; prescription retinoids or urea

conditions.name: Xerosis (Dry Skin)
conditions.description: Abnormally dry skin due to reduced water content in stratum corneum. Can be caused by environmental factors, aging, or underlying skin conditions.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: In darker skin, xerosis is more visible ('ashy skin'); may be mistaken for other conditions; barrier repair is essential

rootCauses.description: Winter, indoor heating, desert climates
Decreased sebum, ceramides, and natural moisturizing factors
Strip natural lipids
Strips lipids and damages barrier
Hypothyroidism, diabetes, kidney disease
Essential fatty acids, vitamins A, D, E

products.name: Ceramide moisturizers
products.description: Restore barrier lipids; essential for xerosis

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: If severe, persistent, or associated with underlying condition

basys_health.name: Thyroid panel
basys_health.description: Rule out hypothyroidism

conditions.name: Seborrheic Keratosis
conditions.description: Common benign epidermal tumor with waxy, stuck-on appearance. Increases with age and sun exposure.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: Higher prevalence and more numerous in darker skin; may be mistaken for skin cancer; cosmetic concern often primary

rootCauses.description: Autosomal dominant in some families
Contributes to development
Very common after age 50

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For cryotherapy, curettage, or laser removal if desired; biopsy if uncertain

conditions.name: Tinea Versicolor
conditions.description: Superficial fungal infection caused by Malassezia yeast, characterized by hypopigmented or hyperpigmented macules on trunk and upper arms.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: More visible in darker skin due to contrast; hypopigmentation may persist for months after treatment; often misdiagnosed as vitiligo

rootCauses.description: Yeast converts to mycelial form under certain conditions
Promotes yeast proliferation
Malassezia thrives on sebum
Increased susceptibility

products.name: Ketoconazole 2% shampoo
products.description: Use as body wash; leave on 5-10 minutes

products.name: Selenium sulfide shampoo
products.description: Use as body wash; leave on 10 minutes

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For oral antifungals (fluconazole, itraconazole) if extensive or recurrent

conditions.name: Folliculitis
conditions.description: Inflammation of hair follicles caused by bacterial, fungal, or physical irritation. Presents as small pustules or papules centered on hair follicles.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: Pseudofolliculitis barbae particularly common in Fitzpatrick IV-VI due to curly/coily hair; PIH and keloid risk from chronic inflammation

rootCauses.description: S. aureus most common
Malassezia, dermatophytes
Shaving, friction, tight clothing
Curly hair re-enters skin; pseudofolliculitis barbae
Pseudomonas folliculitis

products.name: Benzoyl peroxide wash
products.description: Antibacterial; reduces C. acnes and S. aureus

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For severe, recurrent, or treatment-resistant cases; culture may be needed

conditions.name: Cold Sore (HSV-1)
conditions.description: Recurrent viral infection caused by herpes simplex virus type 1. Characterized by grouped vesicles on orolabial area that crust and heal over 7-10 days.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation may occur after lesion heals, especially in darker skin

rootCauses.description: Latent virus in trigeminal ganglion reactivates
Stress, illness, UV exposure trigger reactivation
Common trigger for outbreaks
Psychological and physical stress
Menstrual cycle, pregnancy

products.name: Docosanol 10% cream (Abreva)
products.description: OTC antiviral; reduces duration if applied early

practitioner.name: Dermatologist or PCP
practitioner.description: For oral antivirals (acyclovir, valacyclovir) for frequent or severe outbreaks

basys_health.name: Immune function assessment
basys_health.description: If unusually frequent or severe

conditions.name: Razor Bumps (Pseudofolliculitis Barbae)
conditions.description: Chronic inflammatory condition caused by ingrown hairs, particularly in beard area. Affects 45-80% of Black men who shave.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: Extremely common in Fitzpatrick IV-VI due to curly/coily hair; high risk of PIH and keloid formation; can lead to permanent scarring

rootCauses.description: Hair re-enters skin after shaving; foreign body reaction
Multi-blade razors cut hair below skin surface
Causes hair to snap back below surface
African descent; curly hair pattern

products.name: Single-blade razor
products.description: Leaves hair above skin surface; reduces ingrown risk

products.name: Electric clippers
products.description: Leave slight stubble; prevents hair re-entry

products.name: Chemical depilatories
products.description: Dissolve hair above skin; alternative to sharging

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For severe cases, keloid prevention, laser hair reduction (Nd:YAG for dark skin)

conditions.name: Excess Sebum & Enlarged Pores
conditions.description: Excess sebum production resulting in oily skin and visibly enlarged pores. Pore size is primarily driven by clogged sebum stretching follicle walls and loss of collagen/elasticity around pore openings with age. While genetic blueprint pore size cannot be changed, the stretched and degraded appearance can be dramatically refined through regulation of sebum, follicular clearance, and structural support.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: Enlarged pores and excess oil affect all Fitzpatrick types. Melanin-rich skin (III-VI) may show more visible sebaceous filaments due to contrast against skin tone. Oil-regulating treatments work universally.

rootCauses.description: DHT is the primary mediator of sebaceous gland activity; testosterone converts to DHT via 5-alpha reductase at the skin level
Spikes in insulin and Insulin-like Growth Factor 1 directly upregulate sebum production via androgen pathway
High glycemic load increases insulin and IGF-1, which directly stimulate sebaceous glands
Dairy (especially whey) increases IGF-1 levels, amplifying androgen-driven sebum production
Abnormal shedding of dead skin cells inside the pore creates plugs that trap sebum and stretch the follicle wall
Decreased collagen and elastin around pore openings reduces structural support, making pores appear larger
CRH/ACTH axis increases cortisol and androgens, stimulating sebum production
Testosterone converts to more potent DHT via 5-alpha reductase, amplifying sebum production signal

products.name: Salicylic Acid (BHA) 2%
products.description: Oil-soluble BHA penetrates lipid-rich pore matrix to dissolve sebum + dead skin cell plugs before they stretch the pore. First-line for comedonal congestion.

products.name: Niacinamide 3-5%
products.description: Structurally reduces sebaceous lipogenesis rate while strengthening skin barrier and improving elasticity around pore walls. Clinical workhorse for oil regulation.

products.name: Topical Retinoids (Retinol, Retinal, Tretinoin)
products.description: Normalize skin cell turnover preventing pore plugs; potently stimulate collagen synthesis to rebuild the matrix around pore openings, making them visibly smaller.

products.name: Green Tea Extract (EGCG)
products.description: Potent topical 5-alpha reductase inhibitor; blocks conversion of testosterone to DHT at skin level, reducing hormonal signal for oil production. Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant.

products.name: Azelaic Acid 15-20%
products.description: Anti-inflammatory and normalizes keratinization; reduces pore congestion and post-inflammatory marks from acne lesions.

supplements.name: Green Tea Extract (EGCG) 400-800mg
supplements.description: Systemic 5-alpha reductase inhibitor; reduces DHT production from inside.

supplements.name: Zinc Picolinate 25-30mg
supplements.description: Modulates total androgen activity; natural anti-inflammatory; reduces lipid content of sebum. Pair with copper 2-3mg.

supplements.name: Omega-3 (EPA/DHA 1000-2000mg)
supplements.description: Anti-inflammatory; improves insulin sensitivity; reduces prostaglandin-driven sebum production.

practitioner.name: RF Microneedling (Radiofrequency)
practitioner.description: Combines mechanical micro-injuries with heat energy into the dermis; triggers massive collagen remodeling to tighten skin architecture around pores; can thermally down-regulate overactive sebaceous glands.

practitioner.name: Laser Resurfacing (Non-Ablative Fraxel or Clear + Brilliant)
practitioner.description: Creates micro-zones of thermal damage forcing replacement of stretched-out tissue with fresh, elastic collagen.

practitioner.name: Micro-Tox (Intradermal Botox)
practitioner.description: Highly diluted micro-droplets injected superficially into skin layer; temporarily paralyzes arrector pili muscles and sebaceous glands, drastically shutting down oil production for 2-3 months.

practitioner.name: Dermatologist consultation
practitioner.description: For severe excess oil not responding to OTC treatments; may prescribe tretinoin, spironolactone, or recommend procedures.

basys_health.name: Fasting Insulin & IGF-1 Panel
basys_health.description: Assess insulin resistance and IGF-1 levels as drivers of excess sebum production.

basys_health.name: Comprehensive Hormone Panel
basys_health.description: Check DHEA-S, testosterone, DHT, and cortisol to identify androgen excess driving sebum production.

basys_health.name: Gut Microbiome Assessment
basys_health.description: Gut-skin axis; dysbiosis may drive systemic inflammation contributing to overactive sebaceous glands.

conditions.name: Barrier Dysfunction
conditions.description: Compromised skin barrier leading to increased transepidermal water loss (TEWL), sensitivity, and inflammation. Characterized by dryness, irritation, redness, and impaired barrier function.
conditions.fitzpatrickNotes: More visible in darker skin as 'ashy' appearance; barrier repair essential to prevent PIH

rootCauses.description: Excessive use of acids, retinoids, or physical scrubs damages barrier
Sulfates and alkaline cleansers strip natural lipids
Pollution, wind, cold weather damage barrier lipids
UV radiation damages lipids and proteins in stratum corneum

products.name: Ceramides 1-5%
products.description: Restore barrier lipids; essential for barrier repair

products.name: Cholesterol 1-3%
products.description: Essential lipid for barrier repair; works with ceramides

products.name: Fatty acids (linoleic, linolenic)
products.description: Essential fatty acids for barrier repair and inflammation reduction

products.name: Panthenol 0.5-5%
products.description: Pro-vitamin B5; upregulates lipid synthesis and reduces inflammation

products.name: Ectoin 0.3-2%
products.description: Extremolyte; stabilizes membranes and provides anti-pollution protection

supplements.name: Omega-3 (EPA/DHA 1000-2000mg)
supplements.description: Essential fatty acids; supports barrier repair and reduces inflammation

practitioner.name: Dermatologist
practitioner.description: For severe or persistent barrier damage not improving with topicals

device.name: LED Red Light (630-660nm)
device.description: Anti-inflammatory; reduces inflammation and supports barrier repair

ingredients.name: Hyaluronic Acid
ingredients.description: Can hold up to 1,000x its weight in water. Pulls ambient moisture into the stratum corneum, instantly expanding intercellular space to cushion skin and soften fine lines.

ingredients.name: Copper Tripeptide-1 (GHK-Cu)
ingredients.description: Gold standard tissue regeneration peptide. Upregulates metalloproteinases that clear degraded collagen and replaces with fresh Type I and Type III collagen. Accelerates epidermal stem cell proliferation, noticeably thickening thinning skin.

ingredients.name: Matrixyl 3000 (Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1 + Tetrapeptide-7)
ingredients.description: Clinical peptide complex. Tripeptide-1 signals fibroblasts to synthesize new collagen and glycosaminoglycans. Tetrapeptide-7 downregulates IL-6, protecting the skin matrix from UV and environmental stress degradation.

ingredients.name: Argireline (Acetyl Hexapeptide-8)
ingredients.description: Mimics a component of the SNARE complex (protein structure required for muscle movement). Destabilizes this complex to limit acetylcholine release, softening dynamic lines around eyes and forehead. Often called 'topical Botox'.

ingredients.name: Matrixyl Synthe'6 (Palmitoyl Tripeptide-38)
ingredients.description: Advanced peptide targeting six major structural skin components: Collagen I, III, IV, Fibronectin, Hyaluronic Acid, and Laminin-5. Particularly effective at plumping skin from within to smooth deep forehead lines and crow's feet.

ingredients.name: Leuphasil (Pentapeptide-18)
ingredients.description: Works synergistically with Argireline via a different pathway. Mimics enkephalins (natural pain/stress modulators) to reduce calcium influx into the neuron, dampening the initial signal before it reaches the SNARE complex.

ingredients.name: Syn-ake (Dipeptide Diaminobutyroyl Benzylamide Diacetate)
ingredients.description: Tripeptide mimicking the neuromuscular blocking mechanism found in Temple Viper snake venom. Targets the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor on muscle cell surfaces, keeping muscles relaxed to instantly soften expression lines.

ingredients.name: Relistase (Acetylarginyltryptophyl Diphenylglycine)
ingredients.description: Anti-elastase peptide. As we age, overabundance of elastase enzyme cuts up elastin fibers, causing skin to sag and lose snap. This peptide inhibits elastase, restoring facial contour definition and skin resilience.

ingredients.name: Lactococcus Ferment Lysate
ingredients.description: Probiotic ingredient obtained from the lysate of Lactococcus lactis. Accelerates skin barrier recovery, optimizes epidermal growth making outer skin layers denser, and improves quality of cellular shedding (desquamation) giving glass-like texture.

ingredients.name: Sodium Hyaluronate
ingredients.description: Water-soluble salt form of hyaluronic acid. Holds up to 1,000x its weight in water, pulling ambient moisture into the stratum corneum. Instantly expands intercellular space, cushioning skin, softening fine lines, and creating a smooth canvas for active peptide penetration.

ingredients.name: PDRN (Polydeoxyribonucleotide / Salmon DNA)
ingredients.description: PDRN is a nucleotide polymer derived from salmon sperm DNA (Oncorhynchus mykiss). It works through multiple pathways: Adenosine A2A receptor agonism, DNA salvage pathway, wound healing acceleration, and anti-aging effects.
ingredients.mechanismOfAction: PDRN is metabolized to adenosine, which activates the A2A receptor on fibroblasts and keratinocytes, triggering collagen synthesis, VEGF expression, and anti-inflammatory effects. Nucleotides from PDRN are recycled into new DNA synthesis, accelerating cell proliferation and tissue repair.

ingredients.name: Panthenol (Provitamin B5)
ingredients.description: Panthenol is a precursor to pantothenic acid (Vitamin B5), a critical component of Coenzyme A (CoA). When applied topically, it penetrates the stratum corneum and converts to pantothenic acid, which upregulates lipid synthesis, accelerates wound healing, reduces transepidermal water loss (TEWL), and reduces inflammation.
ingredients.mechanismOfAction: Panthenol penetrates the stratum corneum and converts to pantothenic acid (Vitamin B5), which is essential for fatty acid synthesis and Coenzyme A (CoA) function. This upregulates lipid synthesis for ceramide and sphingolipid production, accelerates wound healing by stimulating fibroblast proliferation, reduces TEWL by strengthening the lipid matrix, and has anti-inflammatory effects.

ingredients.name: Ectoin
ingredients.description: Ectoin is a small amino acid derivative (extremolyte) produced by extremophilic bacteria to survive extreme environments. It works via kosmotropic effect, membrane stabilization, anti-inflammatory effects, anti-pollution protection, and DNA protection.
ingredients.mechanismOfAction: Ectoin structures water molecules around proteins and cell membranes, creating a protective hydration shell. It forms a protective layer around phospholipid bilayers, preventing lipid peroxidation and maintaining membrane integrity. It inhibits UVA-induced AP-1 activation and reduces MMP-1 expression. It prevents particulate matter (PM2.5) from penetrating skin and causing oxidative damage, and protects Langerhans cells from UV-induced DNA damage.

evidenceLevels.A: Strong evidence - Multiple RCTs/meta-analyses with consistent results
evidenceLevels.A-: Strong evidence - Multiple RCTs with minor limitations
evidenceLevels.B: Moderate evidence - Limited RCTs, observational studies
evidenceLevels.C: Emerging evidence - Small studies, case series

severityDefinitions.mild: Limited extent; minimal impact on quality of life; manageable with OTC/self-care
severityDefinitions.moderate: Moderate extent; notable impact on quality of life; may require prescription treatment
severityDefinitions.severe: Extensive involvement; significant impact on quality of life; requires specialist care
severityDefinitions.nodulocystic: Deep, painful cystic lesions; high scarring risk; requires aggressive treatment

zoneDefinitions.t-zone: Forehead, nose, and chin area - typically oilier; common for acne and blackheads
zoneDefinitions.cheeks: Lateral facial areas - common for rosacea, dryness, and hyperpigmentation
zoneDefinitions.forehead: Upper face above eyebrows - common for acne, wrinkles, and melasma
zoneDefinitions.chin: Lower face below mouth - common for hormonal acne, dryness, and melasma
zoneDefinitions.nose: Central facial feature - common for blackheads, rosacea, and sebaceous filaments
zoneDefinitions.jawline: Lower face border - common for hormonal acne and cystic acne
zoneDefinitions.neck: Area below jaw to clavicles - common for acne, aging, and razor bumps
zoneDefinitions.scalp: Skin covering the cranium - common for seborrheic-dermatitis, psoriasis, and hair loss
zoneDefinitions.chest: Thoracic area - common for acne, sun damage, and folliculitis
zoneDefinitions.back: Dorsal trunk area - common for acne, folliculitis, and fungal acne
zoneDefinitions.hands: Dorsal and palmar surfaces - common for sun damage, dryness, and actinic keratosis
zoneDefinitions.upper-arms: Brachial area - common for keratosis-pilaris and dryness

I-III.description: Fair skin that burns easily; lower melanin content
I-III.treatmentConsiderations: Can tolerate higher-strength actives; aggressive treatments generally safe; high skin cancer risk

IV.description: Olive to light brown skin; moderate melanin
IV.treatmentConsiderations: Caution with aggressive treatments; patch test new actives; strict photoprotection

V.treatmentConsiderations: Conservative approach; lower concentrations; PIH prevention paramount; physical sunscreen

VI.description: Dark brown to black skin; very high melanin
VI.treatmentConsiderations: Most conservative approach; PIH prevention is primary; physical sunscreen mandatory; professional treatments by experienced providers only

sleep.description: Sleep is essential for skin repair, collagen synthesis, and barrier function
sleep.recommendation: 7-9 hours of quality sleep; side sleeping increases wrinkle formation
sleep.skinImpact: Growth hormone release during sleep supports repair; cortisol increases with poor sleep

stress.description: Chronic stress increases cortisol, which degrades collagen and triggers inflammation
stress.recommendation: Stress management techniques; cortisol testing if chronic stress
stress.skinImpact: Increased MMP-1, MMP-3, MMP-9; collagen breakdown; triggers acne, eczema, psoriasis, melasma

diet.description: Diet significantly impacts skin health through inflammation, glycation, and nutrient supply
diet.recommendation: Anti-inflammatory diet; low glycemic index; adequate protein; omega-3 fatty acids
diet.skinImpact: High glycemic load worsens acne; dairy may trigger acne in some; antioxidants protect against photoaging

exercise.description: Regular exercise improves circulation, reduces stress, and supports skin health
exercise.recommendation: 150 minutes moderate exercise weekly; wash face after sweating
exercise.skinImpact: Improved blood flow delivers nutrients; sweat can trigger acne if not washed off

sunProtection.description: UV radiation is the primary driver of photoaging, skin cancer, and worsens many conditions
sunProtection.recommendation: Daily SPF 30+; reapply every 2 hours outdoors; seek shade; wear protective clothing
sunProtection.skinImpact: UVA causes photoaging; UVB causes sunburn; both contribute to skin cancer; UV worsens PIH, melasma

smoking.description: Smoking accelerates skin aging, impairs wound healing, and worsens many skin conditions
smoking.skinImpact: Reduces blood flow; generates ROS; degrades collagen; worsens psoriasis, acne, wound healing

alcohol.description: Excessive alcohol dehydrates skin, increases inflammation, and worsens many skin conditions
alcohol.recommendation: Limit alcohol; avoid if rosacea triggers
alcohol.skinImpact: Dehydration; vasodilation (worsens rosacea); increases psoriasis severity

gutSkinAxis.description: The gut-skin axis describes the bidirectional relationship between gut microbiome and skin health
mechanisms: Direct microbial metabolite effects on skin
gutSkinAxis.supportingEvidence: Strong emerging evidence for gut microbiome interventions improving skin conditions

hormoneSkinConnection.description: Hormones significantly impact skin through androgens, estrogens, cortisol, and thyroid hormones

androgens.effect: Increase sebum production; trigger acne; contribute to hirsutism

estrogens.effect: Increase collagen, hyaluronic acid, skin thickness; improve barrier function

cortisol.effect: Degrades collagen (MMP activation); triggers inflammation; worsens acne, eczema, psoriasis, melasma

thyroid.effect: Regulates skin metabolism, hydration, and cell turnover

insulin.effect: Insulin resistance increases androgens and IGF-1, worsening acne and skin aging
insulin.management: Low glycemic diet, inositol, metformin if indicated

zincForAcne.cautions: Take with copper 2-3mg to prevent deficiency; nausea if empty stomach

omega3ForInflammation.mechanism: Reduces PGE2, LTB4; anti-inflammatory; improves insulin sensitivity
omega3ForInflammation.cautions: May increase bleeding time at high doses; refrigerate to prevent oxidation

vitaminDForSkin.mechanism: Immune modulation; antimicrobial peptides; barrier function
vitaminDForSkin.cautions: Test levels first; toxicity rare but possible at very high doses

probioticsForEczema.mechanism: Gut-skin axis; immune modulation; barrier support
probioticsForEczema.cautions: Start low to avoid GI side effects; choose reputable brands

spearmintForHormonalAcne.dose: 2 cups spearmint tea daily OR 400mg extract
spearmintForHormonalAcne.cautions: May affect menstrual cycle; avoid in pregnancy

inositolForPCOS.cautions: Generally well-tolerated; may cause mild GI upset

collagenForAging.mechanism: Provides amino acids for collagen synthesis; may stimulate fibroblasts
collagenForAging.cautions: Source matters (marine, bovine, porcine); check for allergies

polypodiumForPhotoprotection.mechanism: Oral photoprotectant; antioxidant; reduces UV-induced damage
polypodiumForPhotoprotection.cautions: Not a replacement for sunscreen; adjunctive only

tranexamicAcidForMelasma.cautions: Contraindicated with history of thrombosis; requires medical supervision

pregnancySafety.firstTrimester: Avoid all retinoids, hydroquinone, high-dose salicylic acid, oral medications unless specifically approved
pregnancySafety.secondTrimester: Continue avoiding retinoids; some topical antibiotics may be acceptable with physician guidance
pregnancySafety.thirdTrimester: Same as second trimester; prepare for postpartum skin changes
pregnancySafety.postpartum: Can resume most treatments after breastfeeding is established; check individual medication safety

metadata.author: SKINgenius Research
metadata.reviewCycle: Quarterly or upon new guideline publication
sources: American Academy of Dermatology Clinical Guidelines
Bolognia JL, Schaffer JV, Cerroni L. Dermatology. 4th ed. Elsevier; 2018
James WD, et al. Andrews' Diseases of the Skin. 13th ed. Elsevier; 2019
